subject: [PATCH] btrfs: don't force DIO writes to be serialized

reviews.author: David Sterba
reviews.summary: Reviewer David Sterba requested more information about the fio script and the block group profile used for DIO writes, indicating a need for additional context before fully evaluating the patch.
reviews.raw_body:
That's quite an improvement. What's the actual fio script you've used?
Also the DIO depends on the block group profile wrt the buffered
fallback so that would be good to know too.

2026-04-22.patch_summary: This patch addresses an issue in the Btrfs file system where Direct I/O (DIO) writes are being serialized due to a missing flag that was set before the transition to the new mount API in 2023. The flag, SB_NOSEC, tells the VFS that the filesystem may have files without security xattrs, allowing optimizations. Without this flag, DIO writes to the same file will be locked exclusively, leading to reduced performance. This patch removes the serialization by not forcing DIO writes to be exclusive locks, resulting in a significant improvement in DIO throughput, as demonstrated by fio benchmark results showing a 59% increase.

reviews.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
reviews.summary: The author acknowledges a significant drop in DIO write performance in Linux 6.8, which was not recovered from, and plans to investigate automated performance testing to prevent similar issues.
reviews.raw_body:
It is. There's a big dropoff in DIO write performance in 6.8 that we 
never recovered from. I'm going to look into some sort of automated 
performance so this kind of thing can't happen casually.

This was on a VM with 8 cores and 8GB of RAM, with a real NVMe exposed 
through PCI passthrough. The figures for XFS and ext4 in comparison are 
both about ~3GB/s.

# cat go
#!/bin/bash
mkfs.btrfs -f /dev/nvme0n1
mount /dev/nvme0n1 /mnt/test
mkdir /mnt/test/nocow
chattr +C /mnt/test/nocow
fio /root/test.fio

# cat /root/test.fio
[global]
rw=randwrite
ioengine=io_uring
iodepth=64
size=1g
direct=1
startdelay=20
force_async=4
ramp_time=5
runtime=60
group_reporting=1
numjobs=32
time_based
disk_util=0
clat_percentiles=0
disable_lat=1
disable_clat=1
disable_slat=1
filename=/mnt/test/nocow/fiofile
[test]
name=test
bs=4k
stonewall

reviews.author: Qu Wenruo
reviews.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that there is an existing solution in iomap to handle bounded pages, which would eliminate the need for btrfs' specific fallback checks and potentially improve performance.
reviews.raw_body:
There is the bounded page solution from iomap already, which will no 
longer fallback to buffered IO but to use extra page copy to make sure 
the final bio won't change its content halfway.

IIRC it's one extra flag and remove the btrfs' specific fallback checks, 
but I haven't yet verified the behavior/code.

Thanks,
Qu

reviews.author: Mark Harmstone (author)
reviews.summary: Author responded to Qu Wenruo's feedback by clarifying that the patch only addresses taking the inode rwsem exclusively, and is not related to serialization of DIO writes.
reviews.raw_body:
That sounds like a different thing - this is just making it so that
we're not forced to take the inode rwsem exclusively for each write.

reviews.author: David Sterba
reviews.summary: Reviewer David Sterba requested that a test case be added to fstests/tests/perf, specifically for the 'pref' tests which are not yet caught up in the btrfs/fstests repository.
reviews.raw_body:
As you've used fio for that, please add the test case to the
fstests/tests/perf. Though the 'pref' tests haven't caught up I'd like
to at least collect them in our btrfs/fstests repository.

reviews.author: David Sterba
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch description does not mention any potential issues with the new mount API and its interaction with btrfs, specifically regarding IS_NOSEC flag and inode locking.
sentiment_signals: lack of discussion on potential issues
reviews.raw_body:
I've updated changelog with the fio script and added the patch to
for-next. We want to get this backported to stable trees, ETA 2 weeks so
we get some coverage.
